The **Mon language**,[[a]](#cite_note-2) formerly known as **Peguan** and **Talaing**, is an [Austroasiatic language](/wiki/Austroasiatic_language "Austroasiatic language") spoken by the [Mon people](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people"). Mon, like the related [Khmer language](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language"), but unlike most languages in [mainland Southeast Asia](/wiki/Mainland_Southeast_Asia "Mainland Southeast Asia"), is not tonal. The Mon language is a recognised indigenous language in Myanmar as well as an indigenous [language of Thailand](/wiki/Languages_of_Thailand "Languages of Thailand").[[2]](#cite_note-3)

Mon was classified as a "vulnerable" language in [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s 2010 *[Atlas of the World’s Languages in Danger](/wiki/Red_Book_of_Endangered_Languages "Red Book of Endangered Languages")*.[[3]](#cite_note-4) The Mon language has faced assimilative pressures in both Myanmar and Thailand, where many individuals of Mon descent are now monolingual in Burmese or Thai respectively. In 2007, Mon speakers were estimated to number between 1,800,000 and 2 million.[[4]](#cite_note-5) In Myanmar, the majority of Mon speakers live in Southern Myanmar, especially [Mon State](/wiki/Mon_State "Mon State"), followed by [Tanintharyi Region](/wiki/Tanintharyi_Region "Tanintharyi Region") and [Kayin State](/wiki/Kayin_State "Kayin State").[[5]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mon_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Myazedi-Inscription-Mon.JPG/250px-Myazedi-Inscription-Mon.JPG)](/wiki/File:Myazedi-Inscription-Mon.JPG)

The Mon [Myazedi Inscription](/wiki/Myazedi_Inscription "Myazedi Inscription") (AD 1113) is Myanmar's oldest surviving stone inscription.

Mon is an important language in Burmese history. Until the 12th century, it was the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of the [Irrawaddy valley](/wiki/Irrawaddy_River "Irrawaddy River")—not only in the Mon kingdoms of the lower Irrawaddy but also of the upriver [Pagan Kingdom](/wiki/Pagan_Kingdom "Pagan Kingdom") of the [Bamar people](/wiki/Bamar_people "Bamar people"). Mon, especially written Mon, continued to be a [prestige language](/wiki/Prestige_language "Prestige language") even after the fall of the Mon [kingdom of Thaton](/wiki/Thaton_Kingdom "Thaton Kingdom") to Pagan in 1057. King [Kyansittha](/wiki/Kyansittha "Kyansittha") of Pagan (r. 1084–1113) admired Mon culture and the Mon language was patronized.

Kyansittha left many inscriptions in Mon. During this period, the [Myazedi inscription](/wiki/Myazedi_inscription "Myazedi inscription"), which contains identical inscriptions of a story in [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali"), [Pyu](/wiki/Pyu_language_(Burma) "Pyu language (Burma)"), Mon and Burmese on the four sides, was carved.[[6]](#cite_note-tarling-7) However, after Kyansittha's death, usage of the Mon language declined among the Bamar and the [Burmese language](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language") began to replace Mon and Pyu as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca").[[6]](#cite_note-tarling-7)

Mon inscriptions from [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati")'s ruins also litter [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). However it is not clear if the inhabitants were Mon, a mix of Mon and Malay or Khmer. Later inscriptions and kingdoms like [Lavo](/wiki/Lavo_Kingdom "Lavo Kingdom") were subservient to the [Khmer Empire](/wiki/Khmer_Empire "Khmer Empire").

After the fall of Pagan, Mon again became the lingua franca of the [Hanthawaddy kingdom](/wiki/Hanthawaddy_kingdom "Hanthawaddy kingdom") (1287–1539) in present-day [Lower Myanmar](/wiki/Lower_Myanmar "Lower Myanmar"), which remained a predominantly Mon-speaking region until the 1800s, by which point, the [Burmese language](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language") had expanded its reach from its traditional heartland in [Upper Burma](/wiki/Upper_Myanmar "Upper Myanmar") into [Lower Burma](/wiki/Lower_Myanmar "Lower Myanmar").

The region's language shift from Mon to Burmese has been ascribed to a combination of population displacement, intermarriage, and voluntary changes in self-identification among increasingly Mon–Burmese bilingual populations in throughout Lower Burma.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELieberman2003202-206-8) The shift was certainly accelerated by the fall of the Mon-speaking [Restored Hanthawaddy Kingdom](/wiki/Restored_Hanthawaddy_Kingdom "Restored Hanthawaddy Kingdom") in 1757. Following the fall of Pegu (now Bago), many Mon-speaking refugees fled and resettled in what is now modern-day Thailand.[[8]](#cite_note-9) By 1830, an estimated 90% of the population in the Lower Burma self-identified as Burmese-speaking Bamars; huge swaths of former Mon-speaking areas, from the [Irrawaddy Delta](/wiki/Irrawaddy_Delta "Irrawaddy Delta") upriver, spanning Bassein (now Pathein) and Rangoon (now Yangon) to Tharrawaddy, Toungoo, Prome (now Pyay) and Henzada (now Hinthada), were now Burmese-speaking.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELieberman2003202-206-8) Great Britain's gradual annexation of Burma throughout the 19th century, in addition to concomitant economic and political instability in Upper Burma (e.g., increased tax burdens to the Burmese crown, British rice production incentives, etc.) also accelerated the migration of Burmese speakers from Upper Burma into Lower Burma.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

The Mon language has influenced subtle grammatical differences between the varieties of Burmese spoken in Lower and Upper Burma.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11) In Lower Burmese varieties, the verb ပေး ("to give") is colloquially used as a permissive causative marker, like in other Southeast Asian languages, but unlike in other Tibeto-Burman languages.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11) This usage is hardly employed in Upper Burmese varieties, and is considered a sub-standard construct.[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11)

In 1972, the [New Mon State Party](/wiki/New_Mon_State_Party "New Mon State Party") (NMSP) established a Mon national school system, which uses Mon as a [medium of instruction](/wiki/Medium_of_instruction "Medium of instruction"), in rebel-controlled areas.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-12) The system was expanded throughout Mon State following a ceasefire with the central government in 1995.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-12) Mon State now operates a multi-track education system, with schools either using Mon as the primary medium of instruction (called Mon national schools) offering modules on the Mon language in addition to the government curriculum (called "mixed schools").[[11]](#cite_note-:0-12) In 2015, Mon language courses were launched state-wide at the elementary level.[[12]](#cite_note-13) This system has been recognized as a model for [mother-tongue](/wiki/Mother-tongue "Mother-tongue") education in the Burmese national education system, because it enables children taught in the Mon language to integrate into the mainstream Burmese education system at higher education levels.[[13]](#cite_note-14)[[11]](#cite_note-:0-12)

In 2013, it was announced that the [Mawlamyine](/wiki/Mawlamyine "Mawlamyine")-based *Thanlwin Times* would begin to carry news in the Mon language, becoming Myanmar's first Mon language publication since 1962.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Mon language in Burma.

Southern Myanmar (comprising [Mon State](/wiki/Mon_State "Mon State"), [Kayin State](/wiki/Kayin_State "Kayin State"), and [Tanintharyi Region](/wiki/Tanintharyi_Region "Tanintharyi Region")), from the [Sittaung River](/wiki/Sittaung_River "Sittaung River") in the north to [Myeik](/wiki/Myeik,_Myanmar "Myeik, Myanmar") (Mergui) and [Kawthaung](/wiki/Kawthaung "Kawthaung") in the south, remains a traditional stronghold of the Mon language.[[15]](#cite_note-:2-16) However, in this region, Burmese is favored in urban areas, such as [Mawlamyine](/wiki/Mawlamyine "Mawlamyine"), the capital of Mon State.[[15]](#cite_note-:2-16) In recent years, usage of Mon has declined in Myanmar, especially among the younger generation.[[16]](#cite_note-ethno-17)

While Thailand is home to a sizable Mon population due to historical waves of migration, only a small proportion (estimated to range between 60,000 and 80,000) speak Mon, due to [Thaification](/wiki/Thaification "Thaification") and the assimilation of Mons into mainstream Thai society.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18) Mon speakers in Thailand are largely concentrated in [Ko Kret](/wiki/Ko_Kret "Ko Kret").[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18) The remaining contingent of Thai Mon speakers are located in the provinces of [Samut Sakhon](/wiki/Samut_Sakhon_Province "Samut Sakhon Province"), [Samut Songkhram](/wiki/Samut_Songkhram_Province "Samut Songkhram Province"), [Nakhon Pathom](/wiki/Nakhon_Pathom_Province "Nakhon Pathom Province"), as well the western provinces bordering Myanmar ([Kanchanaburi](/wiki/Kanchanaburi "Kanchanaburi"), [Phetchaburi](/wiki/Phetchaburi "Phetchaburi"), [Prachuap Khiri Khan](/wiki/Prachuap_Khiri_Khan "Prachuap Khiri Khan"), and [Ratchaburi](/wiki/Ratchaburi "Ratchaburi")).[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18) A small ethnic group in Thailand speak a language closely related to Mon, called [Nyah Kur](/wiki/Nyah_Kur_language "Nyah Kur language"). They are descendants of the Mon-speaking [Dvaravati](/wiki/Dvaravati "Dvaravati") kingdom.[[19]](#cite_note-:4-20)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mon_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Dialects")]

Mon has three primary dialects in Burma, coming from the various regions the Mon inhabit. They are the Central (areas surrounding [Mottama](/wiki/Mottama "Mottama") and [Mawlamyine](/wiki/Mawlamyine "Mawlamyine")), [Bago](/wiki/Bago,_Myanmar "Bago, Myanmar"), and [Ye](/wiki/Ye,_Mon_State "Ye, Mon State") dialects.[[17]](#cite_note-:3-18) All are mutually intelligible. *Ethnologue* lists Mon dialects as Martaban-Moulmein (Central Mon, Mon Te), Pegu (Mon Tang, Northern Mon), and Ye (Mon Nya, Southern Mon), with high mutual intelligibility among them.

Thai Mon has some differences from the Burmese dialects of Mon, but they are mutually intelligible. The Thai varieties of Mon are considered "severely endangered."[[19]](#cite_note-:4-20)

### Vocalic register

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mon_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Vocalic register")]

Unlike the surrounding Burmese and [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") languages, Mon is not a [tonal language](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"). As in many Mon–Khmer languages, Mon uses a vowel-phonation or vowel-[register](/wiki/Register_(phonology) "Register (phonology)") system in which the quality of voice in pronouncing the vowel is phonemic. There are two registers in Mon:

1. Clear (modal) voice, analyzed by various linguists as ranging from ordinary to [creaky](/wiki/Creaky_voice "Creaky voice")
2. [Breathy voice](/wiki/Breathy_voice "Breathy voice"), vowels have a distinct breathy quality

One study involving speakers of a Mon dialect in Thailand found that in some syllabic environments, words with a breathy voice vowel are significantly lower in pitch than similar words with a clear vowel counterpart.[[20]](#cite_note-thongkum-21) While difference in pitch in certain environments was found to be significant, there are no [minimal pairs](/wiki/Minimal_pair "Minimal pair") that are distinguished solely by pitch. The contrastive mechanism is the vowel phonation.

In the examples below, breathy voice is marked with under-diaeresis.

### Verbs and verb phrases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Mon_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Verbs and verb phrases")]

Mon verbs do not inflect for person. Tense is shown through particles.

Some verbs have a morphological causative, which is most frequently a /pə-/ prefix (Pan Hla 1989:29):

| khaɨŋ | to be firm | pəkhaɨŋ | to make firm |
| tɛm | to know | pətɛm | to inform |
